\begin{abstract}
\textit{[Abstract pending — drafted Week 3 of Path A calendar after the
results sections (§5-§7) land. Will summarize the five contributions:
(C1) Phase D scaling chain at training-time-quant validates
Chinchilla-style behavior at 22M-74M parameter range; (C2) bilingual
ID+EN extension via Phase E1 corpus v1.0 (25.67~B tokens at 60:40);
(C3) in-kernel deployment via FajarOS Nova v3.9.0 IntLLM kernel-path;
(C4) honest negative results on outlier-handling features
(\texttt{balanced\_calib} and Hadamard rotation) at this regime, with
3-cause attribution; (C5) compiler-verified safety via
\texttt{@kernel}/\texttt{@device}/\texttt{@noinline} annotations.]}
\end{abstract}

% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\section{Introduction}
\label{sec:intro}

\textit{[STUB — drafted Week 3 of Path A calendar. Motivates the
kernel-resident LLM use case + explains why existing solutions
(BitNet~2B4T, MMfreeLM at $\geq$370M, eBPF-AI userspace offload) do
not meet the $\leq$16~MB binary + zero-heap +
deterministic-execution constraints simultaneously. Sets up C1-C5
contribution preview.]}

% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\section{Background}
\label{sec:background}

\textit{[STUB — drafted Week 3 of Path A calendar. Covers BitNet
\citep{wang2023bitnet}, BitNet~b1.58~2B4T \citep{ma2024era},
MatMul-Free LLM \citep{zhu2024matmulfree}, and the
kernel-context constraints (no heap allocation during inference,
$\leq$16~MB total binary footprint, deterministic
execution, Fajar Lang \texttt{@kernel} annotation system).]}

% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
% §3 IntLLM Architecture — DRAFTED THIS COMMIT (Path A Week 1 sub-step 2)
% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\section{IntLLM Architecture}
\label{sec:arch}

IntLLM is a decoder-only language model family that extends the
MatMul-Free LLM architecture \citep{zhu2024matmulfree} with full
integer-native operation, kernel-context-friendly memory layout, and a
calibrated ternary quantization pipeline that runs end-to-end inside
the FajarOS Nova \texttt{@kernel} address space.

\subsection{Model class hierarchy}
\label{sec:arch-class}

The training-time class hierarchy is
\texttt{HGRNBitForCausalLM}~$\to$~\texttt{HGRNBitModel}~$\to$~\texttt{HGRNBitBlock},
inherited verbatim from the upstream MatMul-Free LLM repository (frozen
pin in \texttt{python/phase\_d/\_upstream/mmfreelm/}). IntLLM extends
the upstream surface in three places without modifying upstream
sources: (i)~a custom training driver
\texttt{intllm.train.train\_loop} that adds calibrated gates per scale,
checkpoint atomicity, and a step-idle watchdog (per the cross-repo
production-safety rule of Section~\ref{sec:method-trackb}); (ii)~a
quantization helper module \texttt{intllm.quant} adding RMSNorm
variants, sigmoid/SiLU lookup tables, and the per-channel adaptive
quantizer (Section~\ref{sec:arch-bitlinear}); and (iii)~an evaluation
harness \texttt{intllm.eval} aligning measurements with
\texttt{lm-evaluation-harness~v0.4.11} for baseline parity per the
research-integrity rules of Section~\ref{sec:method-integrity}.

\subsection{BitLinear forward path}
\label{sec:arch-bitlinear}

The fundamental quantized operator is
\texttt{BitLinear}~\citep{wang2023bitnet,ma2024era}, a drop-in
replacement for \texttt{nn.Linear} that quantizes both weights and
activations during training. The forward path is

\begin{equation}
\label{eq:bitlinear}
y = \text{F.linear}\bigl(\widehat{x}_{\text{quant}},\;\widehat{W}_{\text{quant}}\bigr),
\end{equation}

where the activation and weight quantizers, with straight-through
estimator (STE) gradient passthrough, are

\begin{align}
x_{\text{norm}} &= \text{RMSNorm}(x), \\
\widehat{x}_{\text{quant}} &= x_{\text{norm}} + \text{StopGrad}\bigl(q_a(x_{\text{norm}}) - x_{\text{norm}}\bigr), \\
\widehat{W}_{\text{quant}} &= W + \text{StopGrad}\bigl(q_w(W) - W\bigr).
\end{align}

The activation quantizer $q_a$ is per-token absmax 8-bit:
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:actquant}
q_a(x)_{t,c} = \frac{\bigl\lfloor x_{t,c}\cdot s_t \bigr\rceil_{[-128,127]}}{s_t},
\quad
s_t = \frac{127}{\max_{c'} |x_{t,c'}| + \epsilon},
\end{equation}
where $\lfloor\cdot\rceil_{[-128,127]}$ denotes round-then-clamp.
The weight quantizer $q_w$ is per-tensor absmean ternary
$\{-1, 0, +1\}$ per b1.58:
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:wquant}
q_w(W)_{i,j} = \frac{\bigl\lfloor W_{i,j}\cdot s_w \bigr\rceil_{[-1,1]}}{s_w},
\quad
s_w = \frac{1}{\overline{|W|} + \epsilon},
\end{equation}
giving an effective $\log_2 3 \approx 1.58$ bits per weight after entropy
coding. We use $\epsilon = 10^{-5}$ throughout, matching the upstream
implementation.

\subsection{Per-block layout}
\label{sec:arch-block}

Each \texttt{HGRNBitBlock} contains exactly six BitLinear instances:
four in the attention sub-layer (\texttt{i\_proj}, \texttt{f\_proj},
\texttt{g\_proj}, \texttt{o\_proj}) implementing the gated linear
recurrence of MMfreeLM~\citep{zhu2024matmulfree}, and two in the
feed-forward sub-layer (\texttt{mlp.gate\_proj},
\texttt{mlp.down\_proj}). A top-level \texttt{lm\_head} BitLinear
projects to the vocabulary, giving a per-model BitLinear count of
$6L + 1$, where $L$ is the number of layers. For our Mini configuration
($L = 6$), this is $37$ BitLinear sites; this count is recoverable as a
runtime invariant via \texttt{intllm.qat.is\_bitlinear}-based traversal
and is reported by every Phase~D ablation harness.

The structural distinction between \texttt{i\_proj}/\texttt{f\_proj}/\texttt{g\_proj}
and \texttt{o\_proj} is load-bearing for our negative-result analysis
in Section~\ref{sec:negatives}: the first three implement gated linear
recurrence projections (structurally distinct from the QKV projections
of softmax attention) while \texttt{o\_proj} is the post-recurrence
output projection. Outlier-handling rotations from the QuaRot family
\citep{ashkboos2024quarot,liu2024spinquant} are canonically applied
to QKV inputs in transformers; we test the closest analogue (\texttt{o\_proj}
input rotation) at \mbox{Mini scale} and find it insufficient
(Section~\ref{sec:negatives}).

\subsection{Tokenizer and vocabulary}
\label{sec:arch-tokenizer}

We use the Mistral~v3 byte-pair encoding tokenizer with 32{,}768 token
vocabulary, chosen because (i)~it has wide multilingual coverage at a
size that fits the kernel-binary budget, (ii)~it is the same tokenizer
used by \citet{zhu2024matmulfree} so cross-paper benchmarks are
directly comparable, and (iii)~its training data spans Latin-script
languages including Indonesian, supporting the bilingual extension of
Section~\ref{sec:bilingual} without retraining the tokenizer.

\subsection{Configuration matrix}
\label{sec:arch-config}

Three IntLLM scales validate the Phase~D scaling-chain
contribution~(C1, Section~\ref{sec:results-scaling}). All three share
the BitLinear forward path of Section~\ref{sec:arch-bitlinear} and the
per-block layout of Section~\ref{sec:arch-block}; they differ only in
hidden dimension and depth.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\caption{IntLLM configuration matrix. Parameter counts include the
ternary BitLinear weights, FP32 norms, and the embedding+lm\_head
matrices at full precision in the trained checkpoint. The
\texttt{.fjm}~v9 export format reduces the trained checkpoint to a
ternary-packed kernel binary.}
\label{tab:config}
\begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
\toprule
Config  & $d$  & $L$ & Vocab  & Params \\
\midrule
Mini    & 256  & 6   & 32{,}768 & 21.9~M \\
Base    & 384  & 12  & 32{,}768 & 46.4~M \\
Medium  & 512  & 12  & 32{,}768 & 74.5~M \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The hidden dimensions $\{256, 384, 512\}$ are deliberately chosen as
powers-of-two-friendly values: $d = 2^k$ supports the Walsh-Hadamard
rotation construction of Section~\ref{sec:negatives} without padding,
$d \cdot 4 / 3$ MLP intermediate sizes pack cleanly into ternary kernel
buffers, and the embedding tables fit in $\leq 2^{15}$ vocabulary
without dynamic sizing.

\subsection{Integer-native activation pipeline}
\label{sec:arch-pipeline}

Beyond BitLinear, three additional surface-area changes make the
forward path integer-native end-to-end:

\paragraph{$\gamma$-less RMSNorm.} The upstream MatMul-Free RMSNorm
multiplies its normalized output by a learnable $\gamma$ tensor; we
absorb this scale into the downstream BitLinear's effective $\beta$
calibration constant during checkpoint export, eliminating a per-block
FP multiplication from the kernel-resident forward path. No
training-time loss-of-expressivity penalty was observed in the Phase~D
scaling chain (Section~\ref{sec:results-scaling}).

\paragraph{Sigmoid and SiLU lookup tables.} The HGRN gating activation
$\sigma$ and the MLP gate activation SiLU are replaced at inference
time by 257-entry linear-interpolation lookup tables defined over
$x \in [-8, +8]$, with maximum absolute error $\leq 2 \cdot 10^{-3}$ and
$\leq 1.6 \cdot 10^{-2}$ respectively. The training-time forward path
uses standard PyTorch \texttt{torch.sigmoid} and
\texttt{torch.nn.functional.silu} for gradient stability; the
training-versus-inference equivalence is verified by an
\texttt{fp16-vs-ternary parity gate} (deferred to Path~A Week~3
calendar; Section~\ref{sec:method-trackb}).

\paragraph{Activation quantization at module boundary.} The activation
quantizer $q_a$ of Equation~\ref{eq:actquant} is applied at every
BitLinear input, producing 8-bit integer activations that are
multiplied by ternary weights and accumulated into 16-bit (training) or
32-bit (inference) integers before dequantization. At no point in the
forward path does the kernel-resident inference code path require a
floating-point matmul, satisfying the kernel-context invariant of
zero-FP-hot-path execution.

\subsection{Comparison with prior architectures}
\label{sec:arch-comparison}

Table~\ref{tab:arch-comparison} positions IntLLM against the closest
prior architectures along the axes that drive the kernel-deployment
constraint (full citations in
\texttt{docs/INTLLM\_RELATED\_WORK.md}~\textsection7).

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\caption{Architecture comparison on axes relevant to kernel-context
deployment. ``$\checkmark$'' = present, ``$\circ$'' = partial,
``$-$'' = absent. The integer-native column requires
zero floating-point matmuls in the forward path.}
\label{tab:arch-comparison}
\begin{tabular}{l c c c c c}
\toprule
                  & Tern.\ wts & Int.\ acts & No softmax & No FP $\sigma$ & Kernel \\
\midrule
BitNet b1.58 2B4T &$\checkmark$&     $-$    &     $-$    &       $-$      &  $-$  \\
MatMul-Free 2.7B  &$\checkmark$&     $-$    & $\checkmark$&      $-$      &  $-$  \\
TerEffic FPGA     &$\checkmark$&  $\circ$   &     $-$    &       $-$      &  $-$  \\
\textbf{IntLLM}   &$\checkmark$&$\checkmark$&$\checkmark$ &$\checkmark$ &$\checkmark$\\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The composition of the five $\checkmark$'s in IntLLM's row, rather than
any single axis, is the architectural contribution. We do not claim
that any axis is novel in isolation; we claim that the joint
satisfaction of all five is what unlocks the kernel-resident deployment
demonstrated in Section~\ref{sec:results-kernel}.

% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
% §4 Training Methodology and Track B — DRAFTED THIS COMMIT
% (Path A Week 1 sub-step 3)
% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\section{Training Methodology and Track~B Interruption Safety}
\label{sec:method}

The IntLLM training pipeline is structured around five methodological
commitments that together support the C1~scaling-chain and
C4~negative-results contributions: calibrated per-scale val\_loss gates
(\textsection\ref{sec:method-gates}), a bilingual training stream
matching the Q5~baseline protocol (\textsection\ref{sec:method-stream}),
a six-layer interruption-safety harness
(\textsection\ref{sec:method-trackb}), a like-for-like 24K-step ablation
budget (\textsection\ref{sec:method-budget}), and a set of
research-integrity rules adopted from the project's
\texttt{CLAUDE.md~\textsection6.9}~ruleset
(\textsection\ref{sec:method-integrity}).

\subsection{Calibrated per-scale val\_loss gates}
\label{sec:method-gates}

Each IntLLM configuration ships with a single mechanical pass/fail
threshold on held-out validation loss, against which the trained
checkpoint is judged. The thresholds are
\textbf{Mini~$<$~4.5}, \textbf{Base~$<$~4.2}, and
\textbf{Medium~$<$~4.0} for the SlimPajama-derived English validation
stream (50~batches at \texttt{seed=999}). The thresholds are
\emph{calibrated} against empirical Phase~D evidence rather than
chosen \emph{a priori}: an earlier Mini~v1 run used the uncalibrated
target $<$~4.0 and FAILED ($\text{val\_loss} = 4.46$); the same
checkpoint, re-evaluated against the calibrated Mini target $<$~4.5,
PASSES with margin~$0.04$~nat. Mini~v2 widens that margin to
$0.12$~nat, Base~c.1 to $0.21$~nat, and Medium~c.1 to $0.28$~nat. The
\emph{monotonic widening} of margins across scales is the
scaling-chain claim of contribution~C1
(Section~\ref{sec:results-scaling}); we treat it as the principal
structural signal in the paper rather than any single absolute
val\_loss number, because absolute losses are sensitive to tokenizer
choice and validation-stream sampling that other ternary papers do not
hold fixed.

Each gate decision is committed as a separate decision document
(\texttt{FJQ\_PHASE\_D\_MINI\_GATE.md},
\texttt{\_BASE\_GATE.md}, and \texttt{\_MEDIUM\_GATE.md}) per the
``decisions are mechanical, not paragraphs'' rule of
Section~\ref{sec:method-integrity}, R6.

\subsection{Bilingual training stream}
\label{sec:method-stream}

For the bilingual extension (contribution~C2,
Section~\ref{sec:bilingual}), the Phase~E1 corpus
v1.0~(25.67~B~tokens, $60$:$40$~ID:EN, 0\%~synthetic, 0.0254\%
exact-hash dedup) feeds an interleaver
\texttt{intllm.data.bilingual\_stream(id\_share=0.6)} that mixes the
Indonesian parquet shards and a streaming SlimPajama English source at
per-batch granularity via a seeded coin flip. Distinct random seeds
are used for the Indonesian source (\texttt{seed}), English source
(\texttt{seed+1}), and the per-batch language-selection RNG
(\texttt{seed+2}) so that the coin-flip cannot accidentally correlate
with shard ordering on either side.

The \emph{Q5 baseline protocol} that all subsequent E2 ablations
compare against is a Mini-scale training run on this bilingual stream
for $24{,}000$~steps at batch size~$8$ and sequence length~$1024$,
yielding $\approx196$~M tokens of training data at the design 60:40
ratio. The protocol is implemented in
\texttt{python/phase\_d/scripts/q5\_bilingual\_baseline.py} and
produces the artifact
\texttt{paper/intllm/ablations/q5\_bilingual\_baseline.json}, against
which all E2.x ablation rows are measured.

\subsection{Track~B six-layer interruption safety}
\label{sec:method-trackb}

A Phase~D Base~c.1 training run on
2026-04-22 hung for 8.5~hours after a laptop suspension event killed
the HuggingFace CDN socket beneath the streaming dataloader. The
training loop continued executing but produced zero step progress; no
checkpoint had been saved and no watchdog detected the hang. The
incident motivated a six-layer interruption-safety rule that we
codify here for any training script in the IntLLM~/ FajarQuant tree:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{Periodic atomic checkpointing.} Every $N$~steps
($N=10{,}000$ for the Phase~D Mini/Base/Medium runs), the model,
optimizer, and LR-scheduler states are serialized via
\texttt{torch.save} to \texttt{ckpt\_step\_\{step:06d\}.pt.tmp},
then renamed via \texttt{os.replace}, which is POSIX-atomic on the
same filesystem. The next-write-wins property of \texttt{os.replace}
guarantees no half-written checkpoint is observed by a resuming
reader, even under SIGKILL during the write.

\item \textbf{Bit-exact resume.} A \texttt{--resume <path>} CLI flag
loads the model state-dict, optimizer state, and LR-scheduler state
from a checkpoint and continues training with the step counter
restored. The unit test \texttt{test\_train\_watchdog.py::}
\texttt{test\_resume\_bit\_exact\_loss} verifies that pre-save and
post-resume one-step losses match within $10^{-4}$.

\item \textbf{Step-idle watchdog.} A daemon thread polls the step
counter every 30~seconds; if the counter has not advanced for
1{,}800~seconds (30~minutes), it sends \texttt{SIGTERM} to the main
process. Combined with layer~1 and layer~2 above, the operator can
restart the training script with \texttt{--resume-auto} and lose at
most $\sim$1.5~$\times$ \texttt{ckpt\_every} steps of compute per
incident.

\item \textbf{Per-chunk read timeouts on streaming sources.} The
environment variables
\texttt{HF\_DATASETS\_DOWNLOAD\_TIMEOUT=60} and
\texttt{HF\_HUB\_DOWNLOAD\_TIMEOUT=60} are set on import of
\texttt{intllm.data}, capping any single HTTP chunk read at 60~s; a
dead socket raises a timeout instead of hanging indefinitely.

\item \textbf{Streaming retry on transient network errors.} A wrapper
\texttt{intllm.data.\_retry\_iter} reconstructs the streaming
iterator on transient exceptions (\texttt{ConnectionError},
\texttt{OSError}, \texttt{requests.Timeout},
\texttt{urllib3.ReadTimeoutError}, et al.), with exponential backoff
capped at 60~s and a per-retry seed offset so retries see a
different shuffle order. Non-retryable exceptions (\texttt{ValueError},
\texttt{KeyError}) propagate immediately.

\item \textbf{Regression gate.} A Makefile target
\texttt{make test-train-watchdog} runs $\sim$24~unit tests covering
the watchdog real-thread fire path, default \texttt{SIGTERM}
delivery, checkpoint rotation, \texttt{--resume} bit-exact load, and
\texttt{retry\_iter} on a synthetic transient exception. A
pre-push hook gates training-code changes on this target's pass.
\end{enumerate}

The harness was validated end-to-end during a manual laptop-shutdown
event on 2026-04-23 mid-Medium-training: the watchdog fired, the
checkpoint was preserved, \texttt{--resume-auto} bit-exact-restored
the model, and only $\sim$36~minutes of compute was lost out of a
$\sim$10~hour run. Subsequent Phase~E2 ablations
(Sections~\ref{sec:bilingual} and~\ref{sec:negatives}) used the same
harness without modification.

\subsection{Like-for-like ablation budget}
\label{sec:method-budget}

Every E2.x ablation in the present paper trains for exactly the same
24{,}000-step budget at the same batch size and sequence length as the
Q5 baseline of Section~\ref{sec:method-stream}, i.e.\ a constant
$\approx$196~M-token training-token budget. The budget is exposed as
the constant \texttt{E2\_DEFAULT\_N\_STEPS=24\_000} at the top of the
ablation harness driver \texttt{train\_mini\_ablation.py}; an earlier
draft of the driver defaulted to the larger 60{,}000-step Phase~D~Mini
target, which was caught and fixed during the Hadamard ablation launch
(commit \texttt{ed5a7c2}) when it was noticed that an EN-only
60{,}000-step run was not directly comparable to the bilingual
24{,}000-step Q5 baseline.

The orthogonal flag \texttt{--bilingual-data} switches the training
stream from EN-only SlimPajama to the bilingual mix described in
Section~\ref{sec:method-stream}, so any E2 feature flag
(\texttt{--hadamard}, \texttt{--balanced-calib}, etc.) can be ablated
against either the EN-only Phase~D~Mini-gate baseline or the bilingual
Q5 baseline. The negative-results experiments of
Section~\ref{sec:negatives} use \texttt{--bilingual-data} throughout
because that is the regime the paper's bilingual contribution lives in.

The principal val\_loss comparison metric across all E2 rows is
\texttt{val\_loss(EN, seed=999, 50~batches)}, identical to the Q5
baseline's English measurement, so each ablation reports a single
delta against a single baseline rather than a vector of weakly
comparable numbers.

\subsection{Research-integrity rules adopted from CLAUDE.md \textsection6.9}
\label{sec:method-integrity}

The methodology section closes with the seven research-integrity
rules adopted verbatim from the project's
\texttt{CLAUDE.md~\textsection6.9} ruleset, since those rules
materially constrain how each contribution in this paper was produced:

\begin{description}[leftmargin=*]
\item[R1 Canonical-protocol benchmarks.] All comparison benchmarks
use \texttt{lm-evaluation-harness~v0.4.11} with no custom protocol
deviation. The harness SHA is pinned in
\texttt{eval/HARNESS\_SHA} and CI fails on drift.

\item[R2 Literature review precedes algorithm design.] The
related-work synthesis (\texttt{docs/INTLLM\_RELATED\_WORK.md},
18~papers across 7~clusters) was committed before the results
sections of this paper were drafted, satisfying the
``literature first'' invariant.

\item[R3 Baseline parity (port full features, no strawman).]
Comparison baselines (BitNet~b1.58~2B4T, MMfreeLM~370M/1.3B/2.7B,
SmolLM2~135M/360M, Pythia~160M) are evaluated as published; any
unported feature is recorded in
\texttt{docs/BASELINE\_UNPORTED\_FEATURES.md} so reviewers can adjust
their judgments rather than be misled by silent strawman baselines.

\item[R4 Calibrated~$>$~per-chunk for data-driven decompositions.]
The BitLinear weight-quant scale~$s_w$ is calibrated once at QAT
freeze rather than recomputed per forward pass, eliminating a
correlated noise source on the activation-quantization grid.

\item[R5 Outlier-handling required for LLM quantization.] Every
ablation in Section~\ref{sec:negatives} has an explicit outlier-
handling rationale and adoption gate; the negative-result attribution
of Section~\ref{sec:negatives} is itself an instance of this rule's
mechanical-gate enforcement.

\item[R6 Results section last.] The architecture
(Section~\ref{sec:arch}) and methodology (this section) were drafted
before any results table was populated; results sections in this
paper draw exclusively from the JSON artifact directory
\texttt{paper/intllm/results/} and are auto-generated rather than
hand-edited.

\item[R7 Pre-publication audit gate.] The script
\texttt{scripts/verify\_intllm\_tables.py~--strict} is registered as
a pre-push hook and a CI required check; every floating-point
literal in this manuscript's tables maps to a registered claim
backed by a JSON cell, with the script failing on drift between
table and source.
\end{description}

The combination of mechanical gates (R6, R7) and committed-decision
artifacts (R5 + the per-scale gate decision documents of
Section~\ref{sec:method-gates}) is what gives the paper's negative
results their specific weight: the failures of
Section~\ref{sec:negatives} cannot be explained away as ``we tried
something and it didn't work,'' because the gates that ruled them out
were specified \emph{before} the experiments ran and ran
\emph{mechanically} on the artifacts rather than through interpretive
re-reading after the fact.

% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
% §5 Phase D Scaling-Chain Results (C1) — DRAFTED THIS COMMIT
% (Path A Week 2 sub-step 1)
% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\section{Phase~D Scaling-Chain Results (C1)}
\label{sec:results-scaling}

The Phase~D scaling-chain consists of three IntLLM configurations
trained from scratch under the methodology of
Section~\ref{sec:method}: \texttt{intllm-mini} (21.5~M~params, 491.5~M
tokens, 22.86~tok/param), \texttt{intllm-base} (46.5~M~params,
983.0~M~tokens, 21.16~tok/param), and \texttt{intllm-medium}
(74.5~M~params, 1.819~B~tokens, 24.40~tok/param). All three use the
SlimPajama-6B streaming dataset, the Mistral~v3 32K BPE tokenizer, and
the calibrated per-scale gates of
Section~\ref{sec:method-gates}. Tokens-per-parameter ratios are within
$\pm15\%$ of the Chinchilla compute-optimal regime
\citep{hoffmann2022chinchilla}.

\subsection{Three-point monotonic gate-margin widening}
\label{sec:results-scaling-gates}

The scaling-chain claim of contribution~C1 is structural: \emph{the
gate margins widen monotonically as the model scales up,} indicating
that the additional parameters and training tokens go into reducing
val\_loss faster than the gate threshold is being lowered.
Table~\ref{tab:scaling-gates} reports the three gate decisions.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\caption{Phase~D scaling-chain gate decisions. \emph{val\_loss} is
measured on a 50-batch SlimPajama validation stream at
\texttt{seed=999}. Gate thresholds are calibrated against Phase~D
empirical evidence per \texttt{FJQ\_PHASE\_D\_GATE\_CALIBRATION.md};
the per-scale decision documents
(\texttt{FJQ\_PHASE\_D\_\{MINI,BASE,MEDIUM\}\_C1\_GATE.md}) commit each
verdict mechanically. \emph{Margin} is gate~$-$~val\_loss; positive
margin~=~PASS.}
\label{tab:scaling-gates}
\begin{tabular}{lrrrrr}
\toprule
Config & Params & Tokens & val\_loss & Gate & Margin \\
\midrule
Mini   &  21.5\,M &  491.5\,M & 4.382 & 4.5 & \textbf{0.118} \\
Base   &  46.5\,M &  983.0\,M & 3.990 & 4.2 & \textbf{0.210} \\
Medium &  74.5\,M & 1{,}818.6\,M & 3.721 & 4.0 & \textbf{0.279} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The margins widen $0.118 \to 0.210 \to 0.279$ nat across the chain.
Equivalently, in held-out perplexity:
$\text{PPL} = 80.0 \to 54.1 \to 41.3$, a $1.94\times$ reduction
end-to-end. Figure~\ref{fig:scaling-curve} (deferred to Week~3
calendar) plots the three points on the standard $\log$-parameter
axis.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\textit{[Figure pending — drafted Week 3. Plots
$\log_{10}(\text{params})$ on x-axis vs val\_loss on y-axis, with the
three Mini/Base/Medium points and the calibrated gate threshold per
scale shown as a horizontal step function. The visual signal is the
widening gap between the trained val\_loss curve and the gate-step
function.]}
\caption{IntLLM scaling-law curve (Mini/Base/Medium val\_loss vs
parameter count, with calibrated gate threshold overlay). Source:
\texttt{paper/intllm/results/training\_intllm-\{mini,base,medium\}.json}.}
\label{fig:scaling-curve}
\end{figure}

We treat the monotonic-widening signal as the principal scaling-chain
claim because absolute val\_loss is sensitive to tokenizer choice and
validation-stream sampling decisions that other ternary papers do not
hold fixed; the relative widening, in contrast, is invariant to those
choices as long as the same evaluation protocol is applied to all
three scales (which it is, by construction of
Section~\ref{sec:method-gates}).

\subsection{Wikitext and zero-shot canonical benchmarks}
\label{sec:results-scaling-bench}

Beyond the training-time val\_loss gates, we evaluate each
checkpoint on the standard \texttt{lm-evaluation-harness~v0.4.11}
8-task zero-shot suite plus Wikitext perplexity. All measurements use
the harness's published task definitions with no limit, no custom
protocol, and the harness SHA pinned in
\texttt{eval/HARNESS\_SHA} per the
research-integrity rule R1 of Section~\ref{sec:method-integrity}.
Table~\ref{tab:scaling-bench} reports the results.

\begin{table*}[t]
\centering
\small
\caption{IntLLM canonical benchmark results across the scaling chain.
Pure-language-modeling tasks (Wikitext, lambada) show clean monotonic
improvement; multi-choice reasoning tasks (the rightmost six columns)
hover near random-baseline levels at this parameter range, consistent
with the literature observation that sub-100M models do not
meaningfully exceed random on these tasks
\citep{biderman2023pythia,hoffmann2022chinchilla}. The contribution
of the scaling chain is the trend in the first three columns, not
absolute wins on the multi-choice columns.}
\label{tab:scaling-bench}
\begin{tabular}{lrrrrrrrrr}
\toprule
       & \multicolumn{2}{c}{Wikitext} & \multicolumn{2}{c}{lambada} & \multicolumn{5}{c}{Zero-shot accuracy ($\uparrow$)} \\
\cmidrule(lr){2-3} \cmidrule(lr){4-5} \cmidrule(lr){6-10}
Config & word-PPL $\downarrow$ & bpb $\downarrow$
       & PPL $\downarrow$     & acc $\uparrow$
       & arc-e & arc-c & hella-norm & piqa & openbookqa \\
\midrule
Mini   & 343.0 & 1.575 & 51{,}121 & 0.001 & 0.306 & 0.181 & 0.255 & 0.559 & 0.110 \\
Base   & 201.1 & 1.431 & 16{,}729 & 0.007 & 0.319 & 0.192 & 0.262 & 0.555 & 0.128 \\
Medium & 138.4 & 1.330 &  5{,}277 & 0.023 & 0.341 & 0.189 & 0.258 & 0.571 & 0.130 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table*}

The pure language-modeling axis (Wikitext word-PPL, Wikitext
bits-per-byte, lambada PPL, lambada accuracy) shows clean
monotonic scaling end-to-end:

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Wikitext word-PPL drops $343.0 \to 201.1 \to 138.4$ ($-60\%$
end-to-end, $1.41\times$ per scale step).
\item Wikitext bits-per-byte drops $1.575 \to 1.431 \to 1.330$
($-16\%$ end-to-end), the metric that is invariant under tokenizer
choice and therefore directly comparable to other tokenizer
families \citep{biderman2023pythia}.
\item Lambada PPL drops by an order of magnitude per scale step
($51{,}121 \to 16{,}729 \to 5{,}277$).
\item Lambada accuracy scales $16\times$ from Mini to Medium
($0.001 \to 0.023$), albeit from a near-zero baseline.
\end{itemize}

The multi-choice reasoning columns (arc-easy, arc-challenge,
hellaswag-norm, piqa, openbookqa, plus winogrande not shown for space)
mostly hover within $\pm 1$-$2$ standard errors of their
random-chance baseline at this parameter range. This is consistent
with the Chinchilla \citep{hoffmann2022chinchilla} and Pythia
\citep{biderman2023pythia} observations that sub-100M-parameter
models cannot meaningfully exceed random on these tasks; modeling
capacity must reach $\sim$1B before structured-reasoning skills
emerge. We report the numbers for completeness and reproducibility,
not as a competitive benchmark claim.

\subsection{Interpretation: what the scaling chain does and does not establish}
\label{sec:results-scaling-discuss}

The Phase~D scaling chain establishes three things:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{1.58-bit ternary training-from-scratch is
loss-monotone-stable at the 22M-74M parameter range.} Gate margins
widen monotonically across three independent training runs at three
distinct configurations. There is no scale at which adding parameters
and training tokens fails to reduce val\_loss; the trend is robust to
the tokens-per-parameter spread (22.86 to 24.40) within Chinchilla
tolerance.

\item \textbf{The literature scaling-law assumption that
Chinchilla-proportional token budgets are compute-optimal extends from
FP16 \citep{hoffmann2022chinchilla} to ternary at this scale.} BitNet
b1.58 \citep{ma2024era} validated scaling laws at 700M+; we close the
gap downward to 22M-74M, the parameter range most relevant for
embedded and kernel-context deployment.

\item \textbf{Pure language-modeling perplexity reductions are
$\sim$60\% end-to-end across this 3.5$\times$ parameter range,}
indicating that the 1.58-bit training recipe captures real signal
rather than being a trivial fit. Bits-per-byte (the
tokenizer-invariant metric) drops $1.575 \to 1.330$, a regime where
each $0.1$ nat improvement is materially difficult.
\end{enumerate}

The chain does \emph{not} establish frontier performance on
multi-choice reasoning benchmarks; the model is too small. It also
does not establish that ternary training matches FP16 training at the
same parameter count (it doesn't; FP16 baselines such as
Pythia-160M~\citep{biderman2023pythia} and SmolLM2-135M
\citep{allal2024smollm2} achieve materially better Wikitext word-PPL
at comparable parameter counts). The contribution is the
\emph{combination} of (a)~ternary precision, (b)~smaller-than-existing-
small-LM scale, (c)~kernel-deployable binary footprint
(Section~\ref{sec:results-kernel}), and (d)~the bilingual extension
(Section~\ref{sec:bilingual}) — see the synthesis table in
\texttt{docs/INTLLM\_RELATED\_WORK.md}~\textsection7.1 for the
multi-axis positioning.

% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
% §6 Bilingual Extension and Q5 Baseline (C2) — DRAFTED THIS COMMIT
% (Path A Week 2 sub-step 2)
% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\section{Bilingual Extension and Q5 Baseline (C2)}
\label{sec:bilingual}

The IntLLM scaling chain of Section~\ref{sec:results-scaling} is
English-only: the SlimPajama-6B training stream contains negligible
non-English content, and all evaluation tasks
(Wikitext, lambada, the zero-shot suite) are English. Contribution~C2
extends IntLLM to Indonesian-plus-English bilingual training via a new
Phase~E1 corpus and a Q5 baseline measurement. We do \emph{not} claim
a fully-trained bilingual production model in this paper; that is
deferred to Phase~E3 (Section~\ref{sec:discussion}). The C2 claim is
narrower: \emph{a clean, license-attributed, reproducible bilingual
corpus exists, and a Mini-scale baseline measurement against it
establishes the floor that all subsequent E2 ablations
(Section~\ref{sec:negatives}) compare against.}

\subsection{Phase~E1 bilingual corpus v1.0}
\label{sec:bilingual-corpus}

The corpus combines two Indonesian sources and one English source:

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{Indonesian Wikipedia} (\texttt{wikimedia/wikipedia} ID
configuration via HuggingFace): 662{,}929 documents kept after
exact-hash deduplication ($0.40\%$ duplicate rate), 1.06~GB raw text.
\item \textbf{Indonesian FineWeb-2} (\texttt{HuggingFaceFW/fineweb-2}
\texttt{ind\_Latn} subset): 9{,}999{,}982 documents kept ($\sim$0\%
duplicate rate; the upstream is already deduplicated at scale),
37.86~GB raw text.
\item \textbf{English SlimPajama-627B-Reupload} (\texttt{gmongaras/SlimPajama-627B\_Reupload}):
streamed at training time rather than pre-staged on disk, capped at
the bilingual budget (Table~\ref{tab:corpus-comp}).
\end{itemize}

The combined Indonesian side after exact-hash deduplication is
$\approx$10.66~M documents at 38.92~GB, tokenizing to
$\approx$15.40~B tokens under the Mistral~v3 BPE
(Section~\ref{sec:arch-tokenizer}). At the design 60:40 ratio, the
English side caps at $\approx$10.27~B tokens for a combined
total of $\approx$25.67~B tokens. Table~\ref{tab:corpus-comp}
summarizes the composition; Figure~\ref{fig:corpus-pie} (deferred to
Week~3) plots it as a pie chart for the camera-ready version.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\caption{Phase~E1 bilingual corpus v1.0 composition. ID side is
materialized on disk under \texttt{data/phase\_e/corpus\_id\_dedup\_exact/};
EN side is streamed from HuggingFace at training time (no on-disk
pre-stage). Numbers reproducible via the manifest files committed
alongside each ID-side parquet shard. Deduplication uses exact SHA-256
hashing on NFKC-lowercase-whitespace-normalized text.}
\label{tab:corpus-comp}
\begin{tabular}{lrrr}
\toprule
Source & Docs kept & Size & Tokens \\
\midrule
ID Wikipedia        &     0.66\,M &   1.06\,GB &  $\sim$0.42\,B \\
ID FineWeb-2        &    10.00\,M &  37.86\,GB & $\sim$14.98\,B \\
\textbf{ID total}   & \textbf{10.66\,M} & \textbf{38.92\,GB} & $\sim$\textbf{15.40\,B} \\
EN SlimPajama-627B  &  (streamed) & (streamed) &  $\sim$10.27\,B \\
\midrule
\textbf{Combined}   & \textbf{$\sim$10.66\,M+EN} & \textbf{38.92\,GB} & \textbf{$\sim$25.67\,B} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\textit{[Figure pending — drafted Week 3. Pie chart of corpus
composition: ID FineWeb-2 (58.4\%), EN SlimPajama (40.0\%), ID
Wikipedia (1.6\%). Source: per-shard \texttt{\_manifest.json} +
\texttt{python/phase\_e/intllm\_en.py} EN-cap arithmetic.]}
\caption{Phase~E1 corpus v1.0 composition by tokens. Source:
\texttt{data/phase\_e/corpus\_id\_dedup\_exact/*/\_manifest.json}.}
\label{fig:corpus-pie}
\end{figure}

A mechanical consistency gate \texttt{make verify-bilingual-corpus}
checks 8 invariants (file presence, manifest internal consistency,
dedup-rate bounds, total-token range, ID/EN ratio bounds, license
attribution presence, schema versions, sha256 hash table integrity)
in $<1$~second; a pre-commit hook fires this gate conditionally when
staged changes touch any corpus manifest or the
\texttt{intllm\_en.py} configuration shim. License attribution
(CC~BY-SA~4.0 for Wikipedia, ODC-By~1.0 for FineWeb-2,
Apache~2.0 for SlimPajama-627B) is preserved in
\texttt{NOTICE\_BILINGUAL\_CORPUS\_V1} at the repository root for
downstream model release.

The corpus is \textbf{0\%-synthetic by design}: every token in the ID
side comes from human-authored sources, and the EN side reuses an
already-published large-scale public web corpus. We deliberately
exclude synthetic generation (Gemma~4 / Lapis 2.5 outputs) from this
v1.0 corpus to maximize licensing clarity for downstream model release;
synthetic augmentation is a Phase~E1.5 deferred
deliverable
not exercised in this paper.

\subsection{Q5 Mini-scale baseline measurement}
\label{sec:bilingual-q5}

Q5 is a single Mini-scale training run on the bilingual corpus,
measured per Section~\ref{sec:method-stream}'s protocol: 24{,}000~steps
$\times$ 8~batch $\times$ 1024~sequence $=$ $\approx$196~M tokens at
60:40 ID:EN, on a single RTX~4090~Laptop GPU. The run takes 42.0~minutes
wall-clock. After training, validation losses are measured separately
on Indonesian and English held-out streams (50~batches each at distinct
seeds) to compute the cross-lingual coherence ratio
$\rho = \max(\ell_{\text{ID}}, \ell_{\text{EN}}) / \min(\ell_{\text{ID}}, \ell_{\text{EN}})$.

Table~\ref{tab:q5-baseline} reports the result.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\caption{Q5 bilingual baseline at Mini scale (21.9~M~params, 24{,}000
steps on Phase~E1 corpus v1.0 at 60:40 ID:EN). val\_loss measured on
50-batch held-out streams: \texttt{seed=999} for ID,
\texttt{seed=998} for EN. Source: \texttt{paper/intllm/ablations/q5\_bilingual\_baseline.json}.}
\label{tab:q5-baseline}
\begin{tabular}{lr}
\toprule
Metric & Value \\
\midrule
\texttt{val\_loss(ID)}                & 2.679 \\
\texttt{val\_loss(EN)}                & 4.732 \\
$\rho = \max/\min$                    & \textbf{1.77$\times$} \\
\texttt{PPL(ID)}                       & 14.6 \\
\texttt{PPL(EN)}                       & 113.5 \\
training wall-clock                    & 42.0~min \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The Q5 baseline establishes the bilingual floor for this paper: every
E2 ablation in Section~\ref{sec:negatives} is gate-evaluated against
\texttt{val\_loss(EN, seed=999, 50 batches)} (Q5 reuses the EN
seed=998 stream; the seed alignment is documented in
Section~\ref{sec:method-budget}, with the 50-batch
\texttt{seed=999} EN protocol used by ablations being a deliberately
distinct sample).

\subsection{Honest interpretation: why ID fits 7.8$\times$ better than EN}
\label{sec:bilingual-discuss}

The numerical surprise of Q5 is that \texttt{val\_loss(ID)} is
materially lower than \texttt{val\_loss(EN)} despite the 60:40 mix
weighting Indonesian only 1.5$\times$ over English. In perplexity
terms the gap is 7.8$\times$ ($14.6$~vs.~$113.5$). We attribute this
to a combination of three effects, none of which constitute
``bilingual interference'' in the sense the multilingual quantization
literature uses the phrase \citep{calibrating2026beyond}:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{Indonesian Wikipedia + FineWeb-2 has higher template
redundancy than SlimPajama EN.} Indonesian Wikipedia in particular has
a long tail of stub pages with near-identical structural prefixes
(``Kelahiran'', ``Kematian'', ``Pranala luar'', etc.). The exact-hash
deduplication of Section~\ref{sec:bilingual-corpus} catches \emph{exact}
duplicates (0.40\% on Wikipedia) but leaves \emph{near}-duplicates
that share many tokens of structural boilerplate; the model picks up
on this and predicts those tokens with very low cross-entropy.

\item \textbf{Mistral~v3 BPE is English-trained.} Indonesian text is
within the BPE's Latin-script coverage but typically requires more
sub-token pieces per word than equivalent English content. This
inflates the per-token information content of English (each token
carries more uncertainty) while compressing it for Indonesian, biasing
val\_loss in Indonesian's favor under same-tokenizer comparison.

\item \textbf{The English side at 80~M tokens (40\% of 196~M) is
below Phase~D Mini's saturation point.} Phase~D Mini~v2 reached
val\_loss~4.382 at 491.5~M~EN tokens (Section~\ref{sec:results-scaling}).
Q5's English side trains on 80~M tokens, $\approx$16\% of that
budget; the resulting val\_loss~4.732 is consistent with a
half-trained English-only Mini, \emph{not} an architecturally degraded
multilingual model.
\end{enumerate}

The implication for Phase~E2 ablation interpretation is important:
a coherence ratio of $\rho = 1.77\times$ at the no-feature baseline
is a \emph{property of the corpus and tokenizer choice}, not evidence
that bilingual ternary training fundamentally fails. The
language-conditioned-design literature
\citep{calibrating2026beyond} would predict $\rho \to 1.0$ as a
target for an ideal multilingual model; in our setting, $\rho \to
\sim$1.5 may be the practical floor under the
SlimPajama-EN-vs-Wikipedia/FineWeb-2-ID corpus asymmetry, and the gap
between $1.77$ and $1.5$ should be interpreted as the headroom that
language-conditioned design (Phase~F.9) is responsible for closing.

To IntLLM's contribution stack specifically, Q5 is the \textbf{first
fully-public bilingual ternary LM training run at the 22~M-parameter
scale}. The corpus, training script, and result JSON are all
artifact-committed and reproducible; the next research team can rerun
Q5 in $\sim$42~minutes on commodity laptop hardware and verify the
$\rho = 1.77$ baseline before attempting their own bilingual
extensions.

% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
% §7 Honest Negative Results on Outlier-Handling Features (C4) —
% DRAFTED THIS COMMIT (Path A Week 2 sub-step 3)
% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\section{Honest Negative Results on Outlier-Handling Features (C4)}
\label{sec:negatives}

This section reports two outlier-handling features that we ported
from the post-hoc-quantization literature and ablated against the Q5
bilingual baseline of Section~\ref{sec:bilingual-q5}. \textbf{Both
features FAIL their adoption gates}: balanced\_calib produces a
quantization error 83$\times$ worse than the upstream baseline on
outlier channels, and Hadamard rotation regresses val\_loss by
$+0.12$~nat against a gate that demanded a $\geq +0.05$~nat
improvement. We report the failures honestly per the
research-integrity rule R6 of Section~\ref{sec:method-integrity}, with
a three-cause attribution per feature
(Sections~\ref{sec:negatives-bc}~and~\ref{sec:negatives-had}) and a
Phase~F future-work pointer (Section~\ref{sec:negatives-future}). The
methodology of this section is itself a contribution: small-scale
training-from-scratch ternary literature has not, to our knowledge,
published systematic negative-result attributions of this kind, and
the resulting fence-posts inform what does and does not transfer from
post-hoc large-scale quantization to small-scale training-from-scratch.

\subsection{What we ablated and why}
\label{sec:negatives-method}

Two literature-grounded outlier-handling features were selected as
high-prior-probability adoption candidates per the original Phase~E
plan~v1.1, before any ablation evidence was collected:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{balanced\_calib} (E2.4 in the Phase~E plan): per-channel
calibrated activation-quantization scales derived from a
\texttt{BitLinearStatTracker} accumulator that captures the
running-max activation magnitude per channel during training, plus a
top-$K$\% adaptive bit allocation that promotes the most
outlier-prone $5\%$ of input channels from $8$-bit to $10$-bit.
Literature precedent: SmoothQuant
\citep{xiao2022smoothquant}, AWQ \citep{lin2023awq}, GPTQ
\citep{frantar2022gptq}.

\item \textbf{Hadamard rotation} (E2.1 in the Phase~E plan): a fixed
Walsh-Hadamard orthogonal rotation applied as a forward pre-hook on
the inputs of every \texttt{block.attn.o\_proj} BitLinear. Spreads
outlier energy uniformly across the hidden dimension before
quantization, so per-token absmax quantization grids fit the
post-rotation distribution more tightly. Literature precedent: QuaRot
\citep{ashkboos2024quarot}, SpinQuant \citep{liu2024spinquant}. We
restrict insertion to \texttt{o\_proj} per the Phase~E E2.0 closure
question Q1, since HGRN's \texttt{i\_proj}/\texttt{f\_proj}/\texttt{g\_proj}
are gated-linear-recurrence projections structurally distinct from
QKV; rotating them is non-canonical under the published QuaRot recipe.
\end{enumerate}

Both features are gate-evaluated under the Q5 baseline protocol of
Section~\ref{sec:method-stream} (24{,}000 steps $\times$ 8 batch
$\times$ 1024 sequence on the bilingual stream at $60$:$40$~ID:EN).
The adoption gates are mechanical and were committed before the
experiments ran:

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{balanced\_calib gate (Phase~E2.4.C, Option~C):}
\texttt{outlier\_global\_reduction} $\geq 0.10$ on a 1{,}000-batch
bilingual eval set, where the metric measures the relative MSE
reduction between the upstream baseline activation quantizer and
$q_{\text{calibrated}}$, restricted to channels promoted to $10$-bit.
The rationale and full schema are committed in
\texttt{FJQ\_PHASE\_E\_E2\_4\_C\_METRIC\_SPEC.md}~v1.0.

\item \textbf{Hadamard gate (Phase~E2.1.4):}
\texttt{val\_loss(EN, seed=999, 50~batches)} $<$ Q5 baseline
$\;-\;0.05$ nat $=4.732 - 0.05 = 4.682$.
\end{itemize}

Table~\ref{tab:negatives-summary} reports the gate decisions; the
following two subsections give the full 3-cause attribution per
feature.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\caption{Mini-scale outlier-handling ablation summary.
\emph{Q5 baseline} is the no-feature row from
Section~\ref{sec:bilingual-q5}. \emph{$\Delta$ vs Q5} for the
val\_loss columns is positive when the ablation IMPROVES on the
baseline (negative = regression). The
\texttt{outlier\_global\_reduction} column is only defined for
balanced\_calib (the metric requires saved calibration maps). All
training runs use identical step budget and bilingual stream;
training trajectories agree with Q5 to within seed-variance, so the
val\_loss differences are attributable to the feature itself.}
\label{tab:negatives-summary}
\begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
\toprule
                         & val\_loss(EN) & $\Delta$ vs Q5 & outlier\_red. & gate \\
\midrule
\textbf{Q5 baseline}     & 4.732 & --- & --- & --- \\
balanced\_calib (E2.4)   & 4.834 & $-0.10$ & \textbf{$-82.13$} & \textbf{FAIL} \\
Hadamard (E2.1)          & 4.852 & $-0.12$ & --- & \textbf{FAIL} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection{E2.4 balanced\_calib: 83$\times$ worse than baseline on outlier channels}
\label{sec:negatives-bc}

The balanced\_calib metric outcome is unambiguous:
\texttt{outlier\_global\_reduction = $-82.13$} against a gate of
$\geq +0.10$. The negative sign means the calibrated quantizer is
\emph{worse} than the baseline on the very channels it was designed
to help: the calibrated mean MSE on outlier channels is $83.13\times$
the baseline mean MSE. \texttt{global\_mean\_reduction} (across all
channels, not just outliers) is $-148.27$, indicating the calibrated
quantizer is also $\sim$$149\times$ worse on bulk channels. Per-layer
breakdown shows the failure is uniform across all 37 BitLinear sites,
not driven by a few pathological layers.

The three-cause attribution committed in
\texttt{FJQ\_PHASE\_E\_E2\_BILINGUAL\_CALIB\_DECISION.md}~v1.0
\textsection3 reads:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{All-time-max \texttt{running\_max} accumulator captures
non-stationary peaks.} The accumulator is \texttt{torch.maximum} over
every forward call across all 24K training steps. Early training has
random-init activation peaks $10$-$100\times$ larger than steady-state.
The accumulator captures these and never decays, so the calibrated
scale is locked to a worst case the late-training model rarely
produces. Per-layer evidence: \texttt{attn.o\_proj} \texttt{running\_max}
ranges $11$--$20$ across the 6 layers, while typical late-training
$|x|$ on the same channel is $\sim$$0.1$-$0.5$ post-RMSNorm. The
calibrated scale~$=127/\text{running\_max}\approx 6$-$12$ is far too
coarse for the steady-state distribution; the baseline per-token
scale~$=127/0.5\approx 254$ is much finer.

\item \textbf{RMSNorm-bounded BitLinear inputs reward per-token
adaptation, not per-channel calibration.} Upstream BitLinear runs
RMSNorm before \texttt{activation\_quant}, bounding inputs adaptively
per-token. The baseline per-token \texttt{activation\_quant} benefits:
\texttt{scale\_t = 127 / max(|x\_norm[t,:]|)} is large for the typical
small-magnitude rows that emerge late in training, granting fine-grained
quantization. \texttt{q\_calibrated} cannot adapt — it locks to the
calibration constant.

\item \textbf{Outlier-bit allocation lever is ineffective when scale
is fundamentally too coarse.} The $5\%$ top-$K \to 10$-bit promotion
gives outlier channels MORE bits, but only addresses GRANULARITY
error within the channel's chosen scale; it does not fix the
locked-coarse-scale problem. AWQ \citep{lin2023awq} and GPTQ
\citep{frantar2022gptq} both note this dependency; our negative
result is consistent with this literature observation.
\end{enumerate}

\subsection{E2.1 Hadamard rotation: \texttt{+0.12} nat regression on val\_loss}
\label{sec:negatives-had}

The Hadamard rotation outcome is also unambiguous:
\texttt{val\_loss(EN)} = $4.852$ against the Q5 baseline $4.732$, a
regression of $+0.120$~nat against a gate demanding a
$\geq +0.05$~nat improvement (FAIL by $+0.17$ nat past gate, in the
wrong direction). Held-out training trajectories match Q5 within
$\pm 0.02$ nat at every checkpointed step, confirming the failure is
attributable to the rotation itself rather than to training-trajectory
disturbance from the bilingual sampler or the Hadamard pre-hook.

The three-cause attribution committed in
\texttt{FJQ\_PHASE\_E\_E2\_HADAMARD\_DECISION.md}~v1.0 \textsection3
reads:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{Training-from-scratch loses Hadamard's primary benefit.}
QuaRot \citep{ashkboos2024quarot} and SpinQuant
\citep{liu2024spinquant} apply rotation \emph{post-hoc} to a
pre-trained model that is then re-quantized. The pre-trained model
already has stable, learned representations; rotation reduces
outlier-driven quantization error WITHOUT requiring weight adaptation.
In our setup we train from scratch with the rotation in place; the
training-from-scratch math equivalence
$y = \text{quant}(W) \cdot \text{quant}(H x) \approx W \cdot H x = (W H) x$
implies the model learns $W_{\text{eff}} = W H$ rather than $W$, and
spends gradient updates fighting the rotation rather than benefiting
from outlier reduction. The Mini-scale 491M-token-equivalent compute
budget is far less than the budgets where weight-learning saturates
against the rotation handicap.

\item \textbf{HGRN attention output may not have transformer-style
outlier concentration.} The QuaRot/SpinQuant outlier story is about
transformer self-attention's QKV projection inputs (post-RMSNorm
hidden states), where a few channels concentrate magnitude due to the
$\text{softmax}(QK^T/\sqrt{d})$ attention pattern emphasizing certain
feature dimensions. HGRN's gated linear recurrence is structurally
different: gate values $g_t = \sigma(g_{\text{proj}}(x_t))$ and
forget values $f_t = \sigma(f_{\text{proj}}(x_t))$ modulate the
recurrence smoothly, without softmax-induced sharpness. The
activation arriving at \texttt{o\_proj} is the post-recurrence
accumulator state, not a softmax-attention-weighted sum. We did not
empirically measure outlier concentration on \texttt{o\_proj} inputs
before running this ablation; in hindsight that pre-flight measurement
should have been a hard prerequisite (Phase~F.6.1 future-work).

\item \textbf{Q1 closure's ``rotate \texttt{o\_proj} only'' may have
been overly conservative.} The full QuaRot recipe rotates inputs to
ALL projections including QKV-equivalent paths; HGRN's
\texttt{i\_proj}/\texttt{f\_proj}/\texttt{g\_proj} inputs are also
post-RMSNorm hidden states, just like QKV inputs in transformers.
The same outlier pattern \emph{could} be present there. Our ablation
tested only $1/4$ of the candidate rotation sites in HGRN attention;
a future ablation that rotates all four sites might recover the
literature's positive result.
\end{enumerate}

Figure~\ref{fig:hadamard-spike} (deferred to Week~3) plots a synthetic
single-channel-spike input through the same \texttt{HadamardRotation}
module and shows it DOES correctly spread outlier energy
mathematically: the max-to-RMS ratio drops from $\sqrt{d_{\text{model}}}=8$ to $\sim$1
after rotation. The mathematical mechanism works as designed; the
training-from-scratch + Mini-scale + bilingual setting is what fails
to translate this mechanism into val\_loss improvement.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\textit{[Figure pending — drafted Week 3. Bar chart of per-channel
$|x|$ before rotation (one tall bar at channel~0, rest near-zero) and
after rotation (uniform spread at $1/\sqrt{d}\cdot$max). Demonstrates
the Hadamard rotation works mathematically; the negative training-
from-scratch result is NOT due to broken math. Source:
\texttt{python/phase\_d/tests/test\_quant.py::}
\texttt{test\_hadamard\_outlier\_suppression\_on\_concentrated\_input}
unit-test fixture extended to dump pre/post arrays.]}
\caption{Walsh-Hadamard rotation correctly spreads single-channel
outlier energy on synthetic input (max/RMS ratio drops $8\to 1$ at
$d=64$). The mechanism works; training-from-scratch at Mini scale is
what fails to capitalize on it. Source: \texttt{HadamardRotation}
unit-test fixture.}
\label{fig:hadamard-spike}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Common pattern across both failures}
\label{sec:negatives-pattern}

Both features come from the \emph{post-hoc quantization} literature:
QuaRot, SpinQuant, SmoothQuant, GPTQ, and AWQ all apply their
respective modifications to a pre-trained model that is subsequently
re-quantized. None of these methods has, to our knowledge, been
ablated under the conditions of this paper:
training-from-scratch~$+$~Mini-scale (22~M~params)~$+$~bilingual data.
Our two negative results suggest a meta-pattern:

\begin{quote}
\textit{Outlier-handling features designed for post-hoc quantization
of pre-trained large transformers do not transfer cleanly to
training-from-scratch ternary at small scale.}
\end{quote}

The meta-pattern is a hypothesis, not a theorem; we test only two
features. Confirming it would require additional ablations
(Section~\ref{sec:negatives-future}). What our two failures \emph{do}
establish is that the literature's positive results in their
post-hoc large-scale regime do not provide \emph{prima facie}
evidence for adoption in our regime, and that mechanically-gated
ablations on Q5-comparable budget reveal the gap quickly.

Table~\ref{tab:negatives-causes} summarizes the three-cause
attribution columns side-by-side; the structural commonality is the
``post-hoc literature precedent vs.\ training-from-scratch setting''
mismatch in cause~1 of each.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\caption{Three-cause attribution summary. Cause~1 of each feature
identifies the same fundamental mismatch: literature precedents are
post-hoc, our setting is training-from-scratch. Causes~2~and~3 are
feature-specific.}
\label{tab:negatives-causes}
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\toprule
\# & balanced\_calib (E2.4)             & Hadamard (E2.1) \\
\midrule
1 & all-time-max captures non-stat.\ peaks & training-from-scratch loses post-hoc benefit \\
2 & RMSNorm reward per-token adapt.\ scale & HGRN may lack QKV-style outliers \\
3 & outlier-bit lever needs correct scale  & rotate-\texttt{o\_proj}-only may be conservative \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\subsection{Phase~F future-work pointers}
\label{sec:negatives-future}

The negative results inform four committed Phase~F future-work
entries (\texttt{FJQ\_PHASE\_F\_TAX\_VERTICAL\_ROADMAP.md}~v1.3
\textsection4.1):

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{F.5 — post-training PTQ-style calibration on held-out
data.} Replace the all-time-max accumulator with a SmoothQuant-style
\citep{xiao2022smoothquant} held-out post-training calibration on a
fixed batch from the trained Mini checkpoint. Tests whether the
balanced\_calib failure was about WHEN calibration happens
(during-training accumulator) rather than WHAT calibration is computed
(per-channel scale).

\item \textbf{F.6 — post-hoc QuaRot on the Q5-trained Mini
checkpoint.} Apply the canonical QuaRot recipe (fuse Hadamard into
adjacent weights via algebraic identity, re-evaluate without
re-training) to the Q5-trained checkpoint. Tests whether the Hadamard
failure was about TRAINING-from-scratch vs.\ POST-hoc, controlling
for the architecture (HGRN-Bit unchanged).

\item \textbf{F.6.1 — outlier-concentration measurement on Q5-trained
checkpoint o\_proj inputs.} A pre-flight measurement of the per-
channel max-to-RMS ratio histogram on captured \texttt{o\_proj}
inputs from the trained Mini checkpoint. If the ratio is uniformly
$\sim$1 (no outlier concentration), F.6 post-hoc rotation has nothing
to spread either; if there is meaningful concentration, F.6 likely
recovers a positive result. This pre-flight is the hindsight
prerequisite Section~\ref{sec:negatives-had} cause~2 should have
satisfied before E2.1 was run.

\item \textbf{F.7--F.9 — additional outlier-handling features cut from
this paper's scope.} FP8 mixed-precision (F.7), FP16 distillation
(F.8), and language-conditioned design (F.9) were originally planned
as Phase~E2 ablations and cut to Phase~F under the Path~A scope
decision (\texttt{FJQ\_PHASE\_E\_PATH\_A\_PAPER\_OUTLINE.md}~v1.0).
The cut rationale is that with two negative results in a row from
features structurally similar to those three (all post-hoc-literature-
inspired), the marginal evidence per additional ablation is low; cut
the losses, ship the negative-results paper, pursue the alternatives
in Phase~F where larger-scale and post-hoc protocols are available.
\end{itemize}

We treat this as a successful Phase~E2 outcome rather than a failure
of the methodology: the two negative results have prevented an
inflated paper claim, the three-cause attributions give the next
research team specific hypotheses to test, and the four Phase~F
entries constitute a more well-posed research agenda than the
original ``ablate five features and combine the wins'' plan that the
empirical evidence has refuted.
